text:
CRK2 modulates  flowering  in Arabidopsis together  with  GLYCINE1

- RICH RNA BINDING PROTEIN 7 (GRP7) 2
- Francisco J. Colina 1# , Julia Krasensky-Wrzaczek 1 , Alegría Pérez-Guillén 1# , Michael 3 Wrzaczek 1,2* . 4
- 1 Institute  of  Plant  Molecular  Biology,  Biology  Centre,  Czech  Academy  of  Sciences,  370  05 5

ORCID

- Francisco J. Colina: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-3168-9336
- Julia Krasensky-Wrzaczek: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-5989-9984
- Alegría Pérez-Guillén: https://orcid.org/0009-0000-0818-8173
- Michael Wrzaczek: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-5946-9060
- Keywords: Arabidopsis, CRK2, GRP7, gibberellin, flowering

Summary

- Timely  flowering  is  critical  for  the  reproductive  success  of  plants.  Plants  adjust
- flowering  time  through  integration  of  multiple  internal  and  environmental  cues.  A
- complex signalling system lies behind this integration, including multiple proteins like
- the GLYCINE-RICH RNA-BINDING PROTEIN 7 (GRP7), linked to the modulation
- of  stress  and  flowering  in  Arabidopsis.  Available  data  show  the  interaction  between
- GRP7 and the receptor-like  kinase  CYSTEINE-RICH  RECEPTOR-LIKE KINASE 2
- (CRK2).  Like  GRP7,  CRK2  plays  multifaceted  roles  in  the  coordination  of  stress
- responses  and  development,  but  its  role  in  development  remains  unclear.  Phenotypic
- analyses of the crk2 and grp7-1 single mutants as well as the crk2/grp7-1 double mutant
- supported the interaction between CRK2 and GRP7. Moreover, exogenous gibberellic
- acid  did  not  recover  the  rosette  phenotype  of crk2 mutants but  as  in sensitive  plants,
- inhibited  GA-biosynthesis  transcripts.  The  pseudo  insensitivity  of crk2 mutant  was

recovered in the crk2/grp7-1

line pointing to a common role of CRK2 and GRP7 in the

- modulation  of  gibberellin  signalling.  Together,  CRK2  and  GRP7  integrate  hormonal
- and spatial signals  required  for  floral  transition. These insights highlight a  previously
- unrecognized layer of regulation in GA signalling and open new avenues to understand

- how  membrane-localized  kinases  and  RNA-binding  proteins  intersect  to  control 34
- developmental timing. 35

Introduction 36

- Timely  flowering  is  crucial  for  plants,  as  it  ensures  production  of  viable  seeds  and 37

38

39

40

41

42

thereby the next generation. Plants adjust flowering time through integration of multiple internal and environmental cues, including energy status, temperature, photoperiod, in order  to  optimize  plant  reproductive  output  in  a  changing  environment  (Andrés  & Coupland, 2012; Wahl et al. , 2013; Song et al. , 2015; Yu et al. , 2015; Kazan & Lyons, 2016).

67

- Arabidopsis thaliana , a facultative long day (LD) plant, has been widely used to dissect
- the  molecular  mechanisms underlying flowering time  regulation.  Photoperiod  plays  a
- major  role  in  controlling  flowering  time  in  Arabidopsis,  (Andrés  &  Coupland,  2012;
- Song et al. , 2015). In addition, flowering in Arabidopsis is fine-tuned by vernalization,
- gibberellin (GA) and the autonomous pathway (AP) (Andrés & Coupland, 2012; Bao et
- al. ,  2020).  The  vernalization  pathway  responds  to  prolonged  exposure  to  cold  by
- suppressing  expression  of FLOWERING  LOCUS  C ( FLC ),  a  central  repressor  of
- flowering,  thereby  enabling  floral  induction  (Andrés  &  Coupland,  2012).  In  parallel,
- GA  and  AP  convey  internal  signals  and  adjust  flowering  time  either  by  directly
- influencing the expression of flowering integrators and meristem identity genes (GA) or
- by  modulating  the  expression  of FLC (AP)  (Andrés  &  Coupland,  2012;  Xiao et  al. ,
- 2015; Bao et al. , 2020). Similar to vernalization, the AP acts through repression of FLC
- (Xiao et  al. ,  2015).  Because  both  pathways  repress FLC ,  the  flowering  delay  seen in

mutants with a defective AP can be reversed by exposing them to prolonged cold, which

- activates  the  vernalization  pathway  (Streitner et  al. ,  2008;  Steffen et  al. ,  2019).  AP
- components, including  GLYCINE-RICH  RNA-BINDING  PROTEIN  7  (GRP7),
- modulate FLC but frequently also have roles in stress responses in plants (Nicaise et al. ,
- 2013; Wang et al. , 2020). Intriguingly, GRP7 also participates in gibberellic acid (GA)
- signalling  (Kim et  al. ,  2008;  Löhr et  al. ,  2014;  Wang et  al. ,  2020),  which  is  tightly
- linked to stress and development (Colebrook et al. , 2014). GRP7 is phosphorylated and
- regulated  by  the  receptor-like  protein  kinase  (RLK)  FERONIA  (FER)  (Wang et  al. ,
- 2020). Signals perceived by the RLK control GRP7 activity and, in turn, regulate the
- splicing-mediated  maturation  of  numerous stress-  and  development-related  transcripts
- (Wang et al. , 2020). GRP7 also interacts with other RLKs (Nicaise et al. , 2013) but the
- functional significance of those interactions remains to be clarified.

RLKs,  such  as  the  CYSTEINE-RICH  RECEPTOR-LIKE  KINASE  2  (CRK2),  play multifaceted roles in the coordination of stress responses and development (Bourdais et al. , 2015). The crk2 mutant combines altered sensitivity to biotic (Kimura et al. , 2020) and abiotic (Hunter et  al. ,  2019) stress with developmental phenotypes, including late flowering  and  small  rosette  size  (Bourdais et  al. ,  2015).  While  the  molecular  role  of CRK2  in  controlling  Arabidopsis  development  remains  unclear,  some  of  its  known functions  may  point  to  possible  mechanisms.  CRK2  regulates  apoplastic  reactive oxygen  species  (ROS)  production  via  phosphorylation  of  RESPIRATORY  BURST OXIDASE HOMOLOG D (RBOHD) during immune responses (Kimura et al. , 2020). ROS also contribute to shoot apical meristem (SAM) maintenance (Zeng et al. ,  2017; Foyer  &  Noctor,  2020).  Tomato  knockout  lines  for SlRBOH1/2 show  accelerated flowering  transition  linked  to  ROS-mediated  modulation  of  the  transcription  factor TERMINATING  FLOWERING  (TMF)  (Huang et  al. ,  2021).  CRK2  has  also  been linked to  regulation  of callose deposition (Hunter et al. ,  2019).  Callose has important roles  in  controlling  plasmodesmal  permeability  and  has  been  linked  to  regulating transport  of  mobile  flowering  signals  like  the  flowering  promotor  FLOWERING LOCUS T (FT) to the SAM (Murata et al. ,  2025).  In  addition, CRK2 has  previously been associated with GA signaling. Exogenous gibberellic acid (GA3) treatment reduces CRK2 transcript  abundance in the  GA  biosynthesis  deficient ga1-3 mutant  (Bassel et al. ,  2011)  and  DELLAs,  repressors  of  GA  responses,  positively  regulate CRK2 transcript levels (Cao et al. ,  2006). Moreover, the abundance of CRK2 correlates with the  GA  biosynthesis-related  transcripts GIBBERELLIN  3-BETA-DIOXYGENASE  1 ( GA3ox1 )  and GIBBERELLIN 20-BETA-DIOXYGENASE 1 ( GA20ox1 )  (Bassel et  al. , 2011).  Interestingly,  GRP7  has  been  identified  as  an in  planta interaction  partner  for CRK2  (Hunter et  al. ,  2019).  Although  no  direct  link  is  currently  known  between callose,  ROS  and  GRP7,  GRP7  is  present  in  plasmodesmata-enriched  fractions (Fernandez-Calvino et al. , 2011)  and  phloem  exudates (Batailler et al. , 2012), suggesting a possible role in symplastic communication. These data also point towards a link between CRK2 and GRP7 in the regulation of flowering pathways. Here  we  explored  the  involvement  of  CRK2  in  the  modulation  of  flowering  time  in Arabidopsis. Through analyses of crk2 , grp7-1 and the crk2/grp7-1 double mutant, we show  that  CRK2  modulates  flowering  time  in  Arabidopsis  through  photoperiod-

- independent pathways, including the GA-mediated pathway. Based on our findings, we

propose a model in which CRK2 impacts flowering time regulation in Arabidopsis by 101 acting as a negative regulator of GRP7, thus integrating signals perceived by the RLK in 102 the modulation of flowering time. 103

Results and Discussion

CRK2 is involved in photoperiod-independent flowering regulation

To  determine the  flowering pathways regulated by CRK2 and whether CRK2  acts in concert  with  GRP7,  we  analysed  the  flowering  phenotypes  of crk2 , grp7-1 ,  the crk2/grp7-1 double  mutant,  and  complementation  lines  ( crk2/CRK2p::CRK2-YFP #114 and #1-22; Kimura et al. , 2020) in comparison to Col-0. Under LD conditions, crk2 exhibits a late flowering phenotype (Bourdais et al. , 2015). Surprisingly, crk2 showed a more severe flowering delay in short day (SD) (Figure S1; Table S1) suggesting that CRK2 could be involved in photoperiod-independent flowering pathways. In SD-grown Arabidopsis, flowering transition relies on endogenous pathways, including the AP and GA  pathways.  Mutants  in  these  pathways,  including grp7-1 ,  frequently  show  more severe delay in flowering under SD compared to LD (Macknight et al. , 2002; Ausín et al. ,  2004; Streitner et al. ,  2008; Steffen et al. ,  2019), suggesting that CRK2 might act via similar mechanisms. In SD, crk2 flowered later than grp7-1 and crk2/grp7-1 , which flowered later than Col-0 (Figure 1a, Table S2a). In addition, crk2 showed fewer rosette leaves than Col-0 at the time of bolting (Figure 1b, Table S2b). This suggested internal factors, including age or carbohydrate levels, rather than external cues in the regulation of flowering time in crk2 . The rosette size of crk2 was consistently smaller compared to Col-0 while rosettes of grp7-1 and crk2/grp7-1 showed a similar size to Col-0 (Figure 1c). This partial rescue of the crk2 developmental phenotypes by grp7-1 suggested that GRP7 and CRK2 interact functionally or converge at common regulatory processes. The  identification  of  GRP7  as  a  component  in  CRK2-mediated  flowering  regulation functionally links CRK2 with the GA and AP pathways, which are both modulated by GRP7 (Kim et al. , 2008; Löhr et al. ,  2014; Xiao et al. , 2015; Wang et al. ,  2020). The AP  and  vernalization  pathways  converge  at  the  repression  of  the  major  flowering repressor FLC. Therefore, the flowering delay of AP  mutants is reversed by vernalization (Streitner et al. ,  2008; Steffen et  al. ,  2019).  Vernalization  at  4ºC for 45 days rescued the flowering delay of grp7-1 and crk2/grp7-1 , but only partially for crk2 (Figure 1d; Table S3). Interestingly, Col-0 and crk2 showed similar FLC abundance in

- 3  and  4-week-old  plants  (Figure  S2a,  b;  Table  S4)  and  vernalization  reduced FLC

abundance  similarly  in  Col-0  and crk2 (Figure  S2b;  Table  S4b).  By  contrast,  the 134 crk2/grp7-1 double mutant showed high FLC abundance similar to grp7-1 (Figure S2a; 135 Table S4a). Together with the similar flowering phenotypes of grp7-1 and crk2/grp7-1 136 (Figure 1; Table S2-3) this suggests that GRP7 modulates FLC independently of CRK2. 137 CRK2 may regulate flowering through alternative GRP7-dependent pathways, e.g. GA, 138 suggesting a partial functional overlap between both proteins. 139

CRK2 regulates GA signaling in Arabidopsis via GRP7

CRK2 and GRP7 have previously been linked to GA biosynthesis and signaling (Cao et al. ,  2006;  Kim et  al. ,  2008; Bassel et al. ,  2011;  Löhr et al. ,  2014; Wang et al. ,  2020) suggesting  that  CRK2  could  potentially  regulate  GA  signaling  via  GRP7.  GA  plays central roles in Arabidopsis development and growth, including flowering induction and cell elongation. Exogenous GA3 treatment promotes flowering and increases rosette size and internode length, due to enhanced cell elongation (Ribeiro et al. , 2012; Löhr et al. , 2014). Treatment with GA3 increased the rosette size of Col-0 but not in crk2 compared to  mock  treatment  (Figure  2a).  By  contrast,  GA3  treatment  increased  rosette  size  in grp7-1 and crk2/grp7-1 ,  similar  to  Col-0  (Figure  2a)  suggesting  an  involvement  of

CRK2 and GRP7 in GA signaling.

GRP7 overexpression plants (GRP7-OX) are sensitive to exogenous GA3 but have been shown  to  contain  reduced  levels  of  GA,  and  reduced  transcript  abundance  of  GA biosynthesis and response genes like ENT-KAURENE  SYNTHASE ( GA2 ) and GIBBERELLIC ACID STIMULATED ARABIDOPSIS 9 ( GASA9 ), respectively (Löhr et al. , 2014). This suggests that GRP7 acts as negative regulator of GA signaling. The crk2 mutant exhibited reduced transcript abundance of GASA9 compared to complementation lines  following  mock  treatment  (Figure  2b,  Table  S5a).  Addition  of  GA3  reduced GASA9 abundance in Col-0 as previously described (Zhang & Wang, 2008), but not in crk2 (Figure 2b; Table S5a) indicating altered transcriptional responses to GA in crk2 . GASA9 transcript abundance did not change in grp7-1 following GA3 treatment, similar to crk2 (Figure  2b;  Table  S5a).  However, crk2 plants  showed  higher  abundance  of GA3ox1 and GA2 ,  which encode GA biosynthesis enzymes, compared to Col-0 under mock conditions (Figure 2c, Figure S3, Table S5b, Table S6). GA3ox1, involved in the final steps of bioactive GA synthesis, is subject to feedback repression by GA (Chiang et  al. ,  1995).  Under  mock  treatment  conditions,  transcript  abundance  of GA3ox1 was higher in crk2 than in Col-0  (Figure  2c, Figure S3b, Table  S5b,  Table S6b).

- Surprisingly, while GA3 treatment did not affect crk2 rosette size, transcript abundance 167
- of GA3ox1 was reduced following GA3 treatment in crk2 similar to  Col-0 (Figure 2c; 168
- Table S5b), suggesting that GA signaling in crk2 is impaired downstream of perception. 169
- Transcript  abundance  of GA3ox1 in grp7-1 and crk2/grp7-1 was  similar  to  Col-0. 170
- Notably,  the crk2 / grp7-1 double  mutant  also  displayed  restored  rosette  size  and  GA3 171
- responsiveness (Figure 2a-c; Table S5). 172
- The dwarf and late flowering phenotype of crk2 resembles GA insensitive mutants such 173
- as gai ,  which also display small  rosette size and  partially vernalization-reversible  late 174
- flowering (Wilson et al. ,  1992), similar to crk2 .  Similar to mutants like gai ,  treatment 175
- 176
- with  paclobutrazol  (PCL),  a  GA-biosynthesis  inhibitor,  show  enhanced  stability  of
- DELLAs,  resulting in reduced growth and late flowering (Serrano-Mislata et al. , 2025). 177
- Interestingly, a positive correlation between DELLA and CRK2 abundance (Cao et al. , 178
- 2006) and reduced PCL sensitivity of crk2 have been described (Bassel et  al. ,  2011). 179
- Thus, we speculate that CRK2 might function downstream of DELLAs and modulate 180
- GA responses partially through GRP7 (Fig. 2d). Available transcriptomic data suggest 181
- that GRP7 targets multiple GASA transcripts  (Meyer et al. ,  2017; Wang et  al. ,  2020). 182
- Therefore,  it  is  tempting  to  speculate  that  CRK2  controls  GRP7  and  thereby  the 183

184

185

186

187

188

189

190

191

192

- maturation of GA signaling transcripts.
- Symplastic transport  is  essential  for  developmental  regulators  like  FT  (Murata et  al. ,
- 2025) and possibly for GA-dependent signaling components. For example, the mRNA
- of the DELLA protein GAI ,  has been shown to move through the phloem and induce
- dwarfism in distal tissues (Haywood et al. , 2005). CRK2 has been proposed to control
- callose deposition at plasmodesmata (Hunter et al. , 2019) and GRP7 has been identified
- in  phloem  exudates  (Batailler et  al. ,  2012)  and  plasmodesmata  enriched  fractions
- (Fernandez-Calvino et al. , 2011). The flowering phenotype of crk2 plants, along with its
- genetic  interaction  with grp7-1 ,  suggest  that  CRK2  could  influence  mobility  and
- processing of mobile transcripts. CRK2 might modulate GRP7 function to coordinate 193
- RNA processing and intercellular transport, thereby integrating hormonal, spatial, and 194
- post-transcriptional  regulatory  layers  essential  for  developmental  transitions  such  as 195
- flowering. 196
- In  summary,  our  results  position  CRK2  as  a  key  modulator  of  GA  signaling,  acting 197
- partly through GRP7 to control development (Fig. 2d). It will be interesting in the future 198

to elucidate the molecular mechanism of how CRK2, together with GRP7, regulate GA 199 responses and flowering time control. 200

Figure  1.  CRK2  and  GRP7  modulate  Arabidopsis  flowering  time  under  non inductive conditions. (a) Flowering time (in days) of Arabidopsis thaliana Col-0, crk2 , grp7-1 , crk2/grp7-1 double  mutant,  and  two  complementation  lines  ( CRK2p::CRK2YFP/crk2 #1-14 and #1-22) grown  under  short-day (SD)  conditions. Statistical significance  was  assessed  by  Kruskal-Wallis  followed  by  Wilcoxon  post  hoc  test. Letters  indicate  statistically  significant  differences between  genotypes  (p  <  0.05). (b) Number of  rosette  leaves  at  flowering  for  the  same  genotypes  under  SD  conditions. Statistical  significance  was  assessed  by  one-way  ANOVA  followed  by  Tukey's  post hoc  test.  Letters  indicate  statistically  significant  differences  between  genotypes  (p  < 0.05). (c) Representative rosettes of 75-day-old plants grown under SD conditions. (d) Ratio  of  flowering  time  (in  days)  for  each  genotype  with  and  without  vernalization treatment  under  SD  conditions.  Pairwise  comparisons  were  performed  within  each genotype  using  Wilcoxon  rank-sum  test.  P-values  were  adjusted  for  multiple  testing using the Benjamini-Hochberg (BH) method (** p<0.05, *** p<0.01). (a,  b,  d) Data are presented as boxplots showing the median, interquartile range (IQR), and whiskers extending to 1.5×IQR. Individual data points are overlaid.

Figure 2. Effect of GA3 treatment. (a) Representative rosettes of two-month-old plants grown under SD conditions and under weekly spraying with mock or GA3 containing solutions. (b) Abundance  of  GA-responsive  transcript GASA9 .  Pairwise  comparisons were  performed  within  each  genotype  using  Wilcoxon  rank-sum  test.  P-values  were adjusted for multiple testing using the Benjamini-Hochberg (BH) method (* p<0.1). (c) Abundance of GA-biosynthesis transcript GA3ox1 Pairwise comparisons were performed within each genotype using Wilcoxon rank-sum test. P-values were adjusted for multiple testing using the Benjamini-Hochberg (BH) method (* p<0.1, ** p<0.05). (b, c) Data are presented as boxplots showing the median, interquartile range (IQR), and whiskers  extending  to  1.5×IQR.  Individual  data  points  are  overlaid. (d) Schematic model for the CRK2-GRP7-based modulation of flowering time in Arabidopsis. CRK2 acts as a flowering modulator in Arabidopsis. In this role, the RLK acts over the GA signaling  pathway,  partially  via  the  inhibition  of  GRP7,  a  known  modulator  of  GA signaling.  CRK2  might  also  modulate  GA  signaling  independently  of  GRP7  and flowering results suggest that the regulation of FLC by GRP7 does not involve CRK2.

metadata.preprint_title: CRK2 modulates flowering in Arabidopsis together with GLYCINE-RICH RNA BINDING PROTEIN 7 (GRP7)

pub_metadata.preprint_title: CRK2 modulates flowering in Arabidopsis together with GLYCINE-RICH RNA BINDING PROTEIN 7 (GRP7)
pub_metadata.preprint_authors: Colina, F. J.; Krasensky-Wrzaczek, J.; Perez Guillen, A.; Wrzaczek, M.
pub_metadata.preprint_abstract: Timely flowering is critical for the reproductive success of plants. Plants adjust flowering time through integration of multiple internal and environmental cues. A complex signalling system lies behind this integration, including multiple proteins like the GLYCINE-RICH RNA-BINDING PROTEIN 7 (GRP7), linked to the modulation of stress and flowering in Arabidopsis. Available data show the interaction between GRP7 and the receptor-like kinase CYSTEINE-RICH RECEPTOR-LIKE KINASE 2 (CRK2). Like GRP7, CRK2 plays multifaceted roles in the coordination of stress responses and development, but its role in development remains unclear. Phenotypic analyses of the crk2 and grp7-1 single mutants as well as the crk2/grp7-1 double mutant supported the interaction between CRK2 and GRP7. Moreover, exogenous gibberellic acid did not recover the rosette phenotype of crk2 mutants but as in sensitive plants, inhibited GA-biosynthesis transcripts. The pseudo insensitivity of crk2 mutant was recovered in the crk2/grp7-1 line pointing to a common role of CRK2 and GRP7 in the modulation of gibberellin signalling. Together, CRK2 and GRP7 integrate hormonal and spatial signals required for floral transition. These insights highlight a previously unrecognized layer of regulation in GA signalling and open new avenues to understand how membrane-localized kinases and RNA-binding proteins intersect to control developmental timing.
pub_metadata.preprint_author_corresponding_institutio: Institute of Plant Molecular Biology, Biology Centre, Czech Academy of Sciences, 370 05 Ceske Budejovice, Czech Republic
